methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Quantum Computing (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Quantum Computing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Quantum Machines is a Series B quantum computing company that has raised $100M+ to date. The valuation analysis draws from a peer universe of 11 comparable companies across quantum computing—3 public (IonQ, Rigetti Computing, D-Wave Quantum) and 8 private firms including Q-CTRL, Quantinuum, and IonQ at various stages.

The stage-progression method is the applicable triangulation approach here. Using $100M in total raised as a baseline valuation proxy and applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical of Series B advancement, the analysis yields an implied valuation of $190M. This method is appropriate given the target's mid-stage positioning and capital history, though it relies on the assumption that total raised correlates meaningfully to equity value at this stage.

The peer-median approach cannot be applied: while 7 same-sector peers exist in quantum computing, the narrow filtering criteria (same stage, adjacent stage, or similar valuation tier) yields fewer than 3 datapoints required for robust median comparison. A broader sector median would risk introducing material noise.

The triangulated valuation range is $162M (low) to $218M (high), with a median of $190M. Key uncertainties include the applicability of the 1.9x multiplier to this specific subsector and the absence of sufficient tightly-matched peer data to cross-validate stage progression results. The single-method support warrants treating this as indicative rather than definitive.
